data.description:
# Parallelize the container fan-out with warm-then-fan-out + fix the centrality budget cap

## Why
The container loop is sequential (`2*N` calls ≈ 27 min wall-clock). Parallelizing fixes latency. But caching
(S1) is write-once/read-many — a NAIVE concurrent fan-out makes all calls MISS+WRITE (≈20x input cost, no read
benefit). So parallelization must WARM the cache first. Separately, the per-plan budget cap scales UP with child
count (centrality), disabling shedding for exactly the large epics that need it.

## What
- Move the container pairings out of the sequential `for crit: for child:` loop into the existing
  `ThreadPoolExecutor` (`pass1.py`).
- WARM-THEN-FAN-OUT: run ONE warming call first, await its first streamed token (the cache becomes readable once
  the first response begins), THEN fan out the remaining pairings concurrently so they READ the cached prefix
  rather than all re-writing it. (Optional: a pre-warm call at gate start.)
- Fix the budget cap that scales with centrality (`sizing.py` `base*(1+centrality)`) so shedding engages for
  large epics instead of being disabled by the very signal (more children) that should trigger it.

## Scope
- In: container parallelization; the warm-then-fan-out sequencing; the budget-cap fix.
- Out: the caching mechanism (S1, dependency); merge (S4); bin-pack (S5).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Container pairings run concurrently in the thread pool (not the sequential loop).
- [ ] Warm-then-fan-out: one call warms the cache before the fan-out; on anthropic the fanned-out calls show
      `cache_read_input_tokens > 0` (NOT all writes) — proven by a test/measurement.
- [ ] The per-plan budget cap no longer disables shedding as child count grows (a large epic sheds as intended).
- [ ] An 8-child epic review wall-clock drops to minutes; `make lint` + tests pass.
data.title: Parallelize the container fan-out with warm-then-fan-out + fix the centrality budget cap
